steps.title: Note the shape of f(x) = 2^x
steps.content: The function $f(x) = 2^x$ is concave up (its second derivative $f''(x) = 2^x (\ln 2)^2$ is positive everywhere). A straight line that crosses a concave-up curve at two points lies above the curve between those two intersections and below the curve outside of them.

steps.title: Apply this to g(x)
steps.content: $g(x)$ is an increasing line that meets $y = 2^x$ at $(h, j)$ and $(k, m)$ with $h < k$. Between the two intersections (for $h < x < k$), the line sits above the curve, so $g(x) > f(x)$. Outside that interval (for $x < h$ or $x > k$), the line is below the curve, so $g(x) < f(x)$.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A: $x > k$ — here the curve grows faster than the line, so $f(x) > g(x)$. Incorrect.
  • Choice B: $x < h$ — here the curve is also above the line (since the line crosses the curve at $h$ from above), so $g(x) < f(x)$. Incorrect.
  • Choice C: $x > k$ or $x < h$ — this is the region where $g(x) < f(x)$, the opposite of what we want. Incorrect.
  • Choice D: $h < x < k$ — exactly the interval where the line lies above the concave-up curve, so $g(x) > f(x)$. Correct.
That matches choice D — D is correct.
